summary: My dad was a Tarascan indigenous person from Zacapu, Michoacán. The Tarascan people, including my family, were farmworkers who worked as ejido-type farmers. They were always in leadership roles in their community, and my grandfather was even a forest ranger protecting the forests in their municipality. Being Tarascan shaped much of my father's early life and struggles.

Zacapu, Michoacán, is where my father's family lived and worked as farmworkers. It was an indigenous village where the Purépecha language was spoken, and my grandmother, who was from Texas, struggled to fit in because she didn't speak Spanish or Purépecha. My grandfather was killed there when my father was eight, which deeply affected our family. Zacapu was the starting point of many hardships and leadership roles in our family history.

Michoacán is the Mexican state where Zacapu is located, and where my father's Tarascan family lived. It was a place of indigenous culture and farming life for us. My grandfather worked as a forest ranger there, and the community spoke Purépecha and Spanish. The struggles and leadership of my family in Michoacán shaped much of our identity before we moved to Texas and beyond.

Lorenzo Guillen was my grandfather on my father's side. He was part Irish and part indigenous, and he served as a forest ranger in his community in Mexico, protecting the forests from illegal woodcutting. Tragically, he was killed by men in the village when my father was just eight years old, an event that deeply impacted our family. I have a photo of him, and he was a striking man who held a leadership role in Zacapu.

Mexico is where my family’s story begins, with my father born in Zacapu, Michoacán, and my mother growing up in Coahuila. It was a place of indigenous communities, land struggles, and migration. After the Mexican-American War, my grandmother’s family lost much of their land, and my parents faced many hardships there before moving to the United States. Mexico shaped our identity and the challenges we faced as farmworkers and migrants.

The Mexican–American War had a lasting impact on my family, especially on my grandmother’s side. After the war, her family lost most of their land in Texas and were left with only a small piece to grow cotton. This loss of land and status affected how they were seen and their economic situation. It was a historical event that shaped the trajectory of our family’s life in both Mexico and Texas.

Texas is where my grandmother came from and where my father eventually moved with his siblings. My grandmother’s family had land there before losing most of it after the Mexican-American War. Texas was also where my father worked on the railroads and where I and my sister Elida were born. It was a place of both opportunity and hardship, especially with the racism and challenges my family faced as Mexican and indigenous people.

In the village of Zacapu, where my father grew up, the Purépecha language was commonly spoken alongside Spanish. My grandmother, being from Texas, could not speak either language, which made her an outsider in the community. The Purépecha language was part of the indigenous identity of my father’s family and their village. It represents the cultural heritage that shaped my father’s early life.

Coahuila is where my mother grew up, in a coal mining area called Nueva Rosita. She had a very difficult childhood there, becoming an orphan and living on the streets until an aunt took her in. Later, my parents got married in Coahuila and bought a piece of land to live on. It was a place of hardship but also where our family tried to build a life together after the migrant circuit.

Nueva Rosita, in Coahuila, was where my mother lived as a child after being abandoned by her family. It was a coal mining town where she survived on the streets until she was taken in by an aunt. The hardships she faced there shaped her resilience and the struggles she carried into adulthood. It’s a place that represents the difficult beginnings of my mother’s life.

My mother's father was a white man who I believe was Basque, although my mother never confirmed it. He had green eyes and light skin, which was very different from my mother’s indigenous features. I met him once when I was a child, and his appearance stood out as very European compared to the rest of our family. This Basque heritage added another layer to my mother’s complex family background.

My maternal grandmother looked very indigenous, and I think she resembled the Kickapoo or Apache people. She was very dark-skinned with straight hair, which contrasted with my grandfather’s more European appearance. This indigenous heritage was a strong part of my mother’s identity and family history. It connects us to the native peoples of the region and their cultural legacy.

My maternal grandmother’s appearance reminded me of the Apache people, with her dark skin and straight hair. She was very indigenous looking, which contrasted with my grandfather’s European features. This mix of Apache-like indigenous heritage and European ancestry shaped my mother’s family story. It reflects the diverse cultural roots in our family.

My parents traveled to Minnesota as part of the migrant farmworker circuit, picking apples and cherries. It was a difficult experience, especially for my mother, who found the migrant life very hard and refused to go back after one trip. The work was seasonal and challenging, and it was part of the broader pattern of migration my father followed to support the family. Minnesota was one of the northern states where they worked before returning to Mexico.

Wisconsin was another state where my parents worked during the migrant farmworker circuit, harvesting cherries and other fruit. It was there that their car exploded at a gas station, a traumatic event that left them with nothing but their two babies. Despite the danger and racism they faced, some local people helped them, which was a rare kindness in their experience. Wisconsin represents both the dangers and occasional kindnesses of migrant life in the 1950s.
